speaker: interviewee
text: And the time is, ten past one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stephanie, you said that you then joined your grandmother and the children in this little village. And where, where were you liberated? Were you liberated in this village?

speaker: interviewee
text: There, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were you liberated by?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russian army. And my grandmother said to us when she seen the German going past our house with a field, a field kitchen, she said to me, that is the end of the war. When the German takes a field kitchen, you will see, the next day will come the Russians. And so it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened immediately after the liberation to you and your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we shifted from this little village to a better place a little further out, a good suburb, a good suburb, a well-known suburb of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived there for a little time, very little time, and went back, but Warsaw was in ruin. It was very hard to find a place to live in. So, we went to the next town, big town, which was Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was there any anti-Semitic activity that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Where?

speaker: interviewer
text: In Lodz or in Warsaw, after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there always was. Where there are Poles, there's anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you suffer anything of that nature?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I still have-- we all still have been on our Polish papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And how long were you in Lodz for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd been working for the government, Polish government. Put Eva to school, because she was still at school age, right? And, I've got a job in the government, in the finance department. And I've-- I pulled Vera after me in there, too. And then I heard of a better job in the government, textile storage. And, I went there as a statistician because that was within my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Still in Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And then, I met a Jewish boy who was in the Resistance in Lodz. His real name was Stefan Berkal, the Jewish. The Polish name in the Resistance, I only knew him as Pavel, Pole. And he was also a Zionist. And he's been helping organizing the smuggling out of Jewish people from Poland, to the German zone, or American zone, or English zone, or wherever. And in Lodz, I met my husband. That's a funny story, too. He came and became friends with a family who has been renting a room in our flat, and he went after Vera. And between Vera and myself, we had decided that it is time now to get a man. We have been battling enough, and especially I had been tired after all this because Vera has been too frightened to put her nose out of the door. It feel all on me to care for the flats and for the papers and for the children, and all that. And I said now, it's enough of it. One of us has to get a man. And Chaim, my husband, was really after Vera. But Vera said, I don't like him. I don't want him. And I got cross and said, if you don't take him, I will. And we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Vera, and Chaim's my husband's, family, they went across the green border into the American zone of Germany. And Grandma, Eva, and myself, we claimed being all born in Germany. We want to be repatriated, and we went legally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Which means that you could have taken a few of your possessions with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say- Where did you go in Germany, in the German zone?

speaker: interviewee
text: First of all, we finished up in a camp in [INAUDIBLE] [INAUDIBLE] or [INAUDIBLE] And from there, a day or two later, they have directed you to DP camps. And we went to Bad Reichenhall DP camp. Which is very, very close to Garmisch-Partenkirchen, where Hitler used to be. While Vera was directed to a different camp. Vera and Nina have been in one camp, in Zaslaw, and we have been in another, but we visited each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you reunited with Roman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandma was with me all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, Roman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Roman. Well, I went after the war into Kalisz to look if there-- there was something still left. And back came from Germany, the girl who has been with Roman in the camp. And she told me exactly that Roman is now in a jail. And she said to me, oh, nothing will happen much to him. But as soon as I got into Germany, into the Bad Reichenhall, I went over to Vera's camp because that was close with Kalisz. And we went to Kalisz and found him in the jail. And immediately started proceedings and got the lawyer and got a rabbi, and all sort of things. And it has taken four years before he got out, but he didn't waste the four years. He studied. He went there as a 15-year-old boy. By the time the final sentence came, he was already 16. And therefore, he was liable to the death sentence. But the judge said to the lawyer, not to me, that he doesn't believe the story the boy is saying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to tell us what he was charged with? What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a shooting during a robbery, a man, a German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was after the war finished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When the inmates of the workers came, when they went on all sort of rampages. Because they've been all hungry, and wanted--

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was finally released?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, after four, years released, and told never to come back to Germany. And he said, he has no intention.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is he now?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel. He went straight to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you, Stephanie, when you went to Kalisz, was there any reaction to Jews coming back, like yourself? Did people think that you weren't going to come back? What had happened to your property there?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people I stayed with-- so they have been my friends there, the one who survived from the Resistance and his family, who also have been beneficiaries from my uncle, they have been very good to me. They take me out of the hotel where I came, and I had to stay with them and fed me and all the rest of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your family home, for example?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived in one of the poshest places. And it was [INAUDIBLE] a Russian, a Russian place has taken it over. Oh, a funny thing was I went to see where I have grown up. And in front of the house was a Russian guard. And when I've been looking around and walked into the backyard, he spoke to me. What do you want to do here? What are you after? And I said to him, I've been playing ball here before you were even born.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you come to arrive in Australia, Stephanie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, while working in Lodz, we contacted our auntie who lived in Turkey from before the war. She migrated from Berlin. Her daughter was married to a bloke who got sent out from Germany to Turkey by his firm. And he's taking his parents-- she's taking her parents with her to Turkey, in Istanbul. And this Auntie Martha from Istanbul, contacted the auntie Rubbie here in Australia. And we got sent landing permits and paid the airfares.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you first arrived in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we first arrived, we were taken to Hazelbrook, to Auntie Rubbie's little vacation house, or whatever you call it. We stayed there for quite some time recuperating from the war. And then we went to work. Since my husband was a farmer from way back, we went on a farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I wanted to tell you about my husband. He was one of ten children. One-- what? That's OK. Just a little bit about your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest girl, she migrated to America before the war, and remembered him only as a blonde little baby with blue eyes, that's all. And all the rest of the family, but one younger sister, perished in the ghetto, in the ghetto in the [INAUDIBLE] or Belarus as they call it now. And Chaim and his sister, they joined the, the Russian partisans. And he was, to the end of the war, with the Russian partisans. When they called him up to the Russian Army, he said no, I had enough. And he gave his hut, his villa where he lived in the village before the war, and his girlfriend was from-- he lived there in this hut to the Major of the Army and got permission to leave, leave for Poland, and came to Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and met you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Stephanie, can I ask you, has the experience of the Holocaust affected the way you've raised your own children? Your two-- yes--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. To this point that I have seen that people who had some faith, no matter what faith, it was Jewish or Christian or any other, had an easier life. I made sure that my children knew that they are Jewish, and that there is somebody up the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how has this affected--

speaker: interviewee
text: But it didn't work out for my grandchildren. Both my children, yes. They recognize that they are Jewish, especially Beatrice. She really keeps to it. Alex is the one who lives here next door. You've seen his little boy. He knows and he recognizes and goes once or twice a year to the synagogue, and he feels Jewish. As a matter of fact, his first wife, he brought her in. She got converted to Judaism. And they had two children, [BACKGROUND NOISE] a Chaim again, and a Rebecca. Chaim is now 23. Rebecca is 21. She was Apprentice of the Year last year, and brings award after award home. And their mother, the girl he brought in, she died at 27 of cancer. Though I've taken over the household. And I've been keeping house for both here and there, and bringing the two children up. They've been only five and seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you say your grandchildren are not--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but when they've grown up, they turned their back on Judaism because they've been ostracized. And because they lived outside the Jewish community, outside Bondi, outside Eastern Suburb, outside the North Shore. When they've been to the Sunday school, because they both have been through the Sunday school in the temple, [INAUDIBLE] temple.

speaker: interviewer
text: The kids have been calling them country bumpkins. And what do you know? You are not going to a posh school, and all these other things. And the rabbi was no help at all. And not very long ago, I had a go at the rabbi, because of this. Because he asked me, why don't the children come to the synagogue? And I told him. It's you who made it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because when they've been kids, and when they have been Jewish dancers and Jews, and the father brought the kids here. And when you've been asked to introduce the kids to other Jewish young people, you didn't do anything. They felt so left out. They finally turned their back on, on it. And there is how it was that this time, he sent me a special invitation to the temple, and all the rest of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And has it affected your spirituality at all, Stephanie?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes and no. Well, I can't believe, I can't bring myself to believe into anything, after seeing and being through all this. It's hard to believe into anything. But as I said, I've seen that life is so much easier if you have a faith, if you do believe. Though I try to instill it, and successfully, in my kids, but not the grand-- not the grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Stephanie, if you had--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my son Alex, after eight years saying that he had the best, so he doesn't want a second best, meaning a second wife. Finally he's seen that I've been just not managing anymore, a full-time job of teaching and keeping two houses, and looking after children. And he finally got married again and he has now a little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Stephanie, is there anything that you would wish to say to the perpetrators of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, as I told you, I come from a mixed stock, so to say. On the one side it's Germans, on the other side is the Poles. And I can tell you only one thing, that the Poles are organic anti-- anti-Semites, and no matter when and where you will go, sooner or later it comes out. While the Germans have been anti-Semitic on befell on-- Hitler has told them to be. That is the difference. A German is not anti-Semitic in himself. He did what he was told, because Germans are very obedient, and when he is told by his superior to do this or those, he will do it. A funny thing, I recall one of my uncles, a German, Arthur Miller-- he was injured during the war and he was always limping. He was a cripple afterwards. But he had an Iron Cross. And when the Hitler's thing came, he used to wear his Iron Cross right here. And all the SS saluted him, of course, not knowing that he's a Jew. And he said to me that one day, he stood in front of a cinema, and the SS has been told to occupy the cinema. And because they are German, because they love order, they all bought tickets before they went to occupy the cinema. That shows you only what the Germans are like. That they will obey the given orders, and the orders.

speaker: interviewer
text: And finally, Stephanie, is there some message you'd like to give young people about the experience of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I honestly don't know because the reaction of young people is so different. Some are really touched by it. Others want to reject it, or simply don't believe it.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] What would you say to those people?

speaker: interviewee
text: To which one? The one who don't believe? Well, things like these sort of tapes will help to make it or things like the film of Spielberg. Because today's youth believes in what they see on the telly or on the films. That is a much better way of showing them what was, than what you just talk. Because when you talk, they will tell you, oh, yes. You make up. But when they see something on the television, that is true. You know today's young people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there anything else you'd like to add, that you would like to say about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: To what? To my personal--

speaker: interviewer
text: Story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I told you most of it, if not all. But most of what I went through, and that I always believed that I will make it. And I still believe that I will make it, despite the fact that I have a steel valve in the heart, and scoliosis in the back, and anemia, and all sort of other things. I'm still part time working. I don't know what else.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I probably know. Thank you very much, Stephanie. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if you have to thank me or if I have to thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: How about a cuppa now?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well that would be nice, but first it'd be nice-- Stephanie, could you tell us please, what this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a Kennkarte, a German document for everybody who was in Warsaw. But it was issued by the Resistance. It's a false one, not issued by Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this allowed you to pass as a Pole. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: 22, Yes, about 22, maybe 23.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Stephanie, could you explain what this is, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a union-- belonging to the union card, when I've been working for the Polish government after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're on which name here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stephanie Bogacka, which was my first assumed name when I went to the Polish--

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was it necessary for you to work under an assumed name after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because of the anti-Semitism in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. Thank you. Stephanie, could you tell us what this is, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a card for an inhabitant of the DP, Displaced Persons camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been between '46 and '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Stephanie, could you tell us what this is, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a driver's license issued by the German local authorities when we've been in the DP camp in Bad Reichenhall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you need a driver's license?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I wanted to drive. There was no--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. And could you tell us about this, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a document for being in France, in Paris, waiting for the migration to Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good, thank you. Stephanie, could you tell us about these two documents, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one here on the left is certificate of religious marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the right. From the right.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right, but on the left front for the two. The right one is here. And that is a marriage-- marriage certificate from Bad Reichenhall in Germany where we have been in the DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you have a religious ceremony in Poland and not a civil one? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I didn't know how long, and when, and where, and what would happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say it was a religious ceremony, was it conducted by a rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: By a rabbi.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not in a shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in the shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much. Stephanie, what is this document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute, you have here my husband, not mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, is it? Sorry. OK stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have exactly the same. That's enough.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, yes. OK, Stephanie, can you tell us what this document is, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a Certificate of Morality, which had to be issued by the local, in this case, the Jewish DP police, before you could apply for migration.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Thank you. Stephanie, can you tell us who's in this photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother, Sophia, with my older sister, Vera.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what time that was taken and where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Krotoszyn, and Vera was here about a year old. So it must have been in 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how do you come by that photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: From my auntie in Turkey.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a photo of my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stephanie, can you tell us who this is, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Bruno Reich, my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, and do you know what year that was taken and where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in Kalisz, and it must have been probably in the 1920s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. And Stephanie, can you tell us who's in this photo, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my husband, Chaim, with our first car.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: Here, on this farm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell us who is in the left-hand photo by the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my daughter, Beatrice, with her two children, Jessica and Caroline.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the right?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Alexander, my son, with his second wife, Judith.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
